figures.caption: Figure 1: Overview of CloudWeb. CloudWeb optimizes cloud- and haze-like perturbations to shift a remote sensing image toward weather-related evidence in a frozen vision-language retrieval space. The hijacked top-k evidence is then passed to the downstream VLM, inducing evidence-grounded hallucinations without modifying the retriever, generator, vector database, or retrieval logic.

Table 2: Generation impact on the strong 303 subset across six downstream VLMs. NW denotes newly induced weather-related responses. G-ASR, ASR, and WHR denote generation-level attack success rate, attack success rate, and weather hallucination rate, respectively. All values except NW are percentages.

Figure 3: Propagation of retrieval hijacking to downstream generation. Clean responses preserve source-scene semantics, while adversarial responses are shifted toward weather-related semantics after CloudWeb redirects retrieved evidence.

Table 10: Multi-target atmospheric semantic results across five retrievers. Target@1 and Target@5 are computed with respect to the corresponding target group (cloud, fog-haze, or smoke-mist). Higher is better for all metrics.
Figure 4: Attention shift induced by CloudWeb. Weather-oriented activation becomes stronger around atmospheric perturbation regions in adversarial queries.
Figure 5: Robustness of CloudWeb under postprocessing. CloudWeb preserves retrieval disruption and weather-evidence hijacking under JPEG compression, Gaussian blur, and resizing.
Table 4: Per-dataset Top5 Changed (%) on Sample100 across the seven benchmark datasets.
Table 5: Per-dataset Weather@5 (%) on Sample100 across the seven benchmark datasets.
Table 7: Mechanism analysis across five retrievers on Sample100.
Table 6: Top-k scaling analysis across five retrievers on Sample100.
Table 3: Hyperparameter configuration of CloudWeb in the main experiments.
